coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted carbon dots fluorescent sensing platform for visual detection of Thiophanate-methyl in fruits and vegetables
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya
Ma, Yanxin
Wang, Hui
Shang, Fei
Yang, Bo
Han, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Trimesic acid and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were employed as precursors to synthesize yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots (Y-G-CDs) by solvothermal synthesis for the sensitive detection of Thiophanate-methyl (TM) in real agricultural products. The Y-G-CDs probe could specifically recognize the TM primarily through π-π stacking. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching of the probe was ultimately dominated by the PET effect, based on the interaction between the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of the Y-G-CDs and the amino group of TM. A strong linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching of the probe and TM concentration in the range of 0–10 µmol/L was observed and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 50.7 nmol/L. Compared to the interference pesticides, the Y-G-CDs probe demonstrated exceptional selectivity toward TM, with satisfactory recoveries of 96.3 % − 104.2 % in spiked food samples. The Y-G-CDs probe enables simple pretreatment, cost-effective, and on-site detection of TM in fruits and vegetables with visual detection of the TM employing a smartphone-assisted sensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanoprobe
Carbon dots
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform
Pesticides
Thiophanate-methyl
Visual detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32060571 ), West Light Foundation, Chinese Academy of Science ( 21JR7RM186 ), Science and Technology Department of Gansu Province ( 23CXGM0001 , 22JR5RM204 , 23CXGM0001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, HR-TEM images, (B) particle size distributions histogram, (C) FTIR spectra of TM, Y-G-CDs, and Y-G-CDs + TM, and (D) XRD pattern of Y-G-CDs.

(A) XPS spectra of d-CDs, High-resolution XPS spectra of elements in Y-G-CDs and Y-G-CDs + TM:（B）Carbon, (C) Nitrogen, (D) Oxygen,(E) Sulfur, and (F) The UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), and fluorescence spectra (FL) of Y-G-CDs.
The values of fluorescence of Y-G-CDs probe at different:（A）excitation wavelength, (B) pH after the introduction of TM, (C)Time.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of Y-G-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TM, the inset: the corresponding images under UV lamp (365 nm). (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of Y-G-CDs and different concentrations of TM. (C) The schematic diagram of capturing and recognizing fluorescent colors on a smartphone-assisted platform. (D) CIE chromaticity diagram of the sensor with different TM concentrations. (E)The B/G value versus concentration of TM (n = 3). (F) The fluorescence intensity change of Y-G-CDs in the presence of TM (1 µmol/L) and other different interfering pesticides (10 µmol/L).
Illustration of fluorescence nanoprobe integrating with smartphone-assisted sensing platform for Thiophanate-methyl.
Comparison of fluorescent sensors with some previous sensing methods for detecting TM.
Detection of TM in spiked samples by the Y-G-CDs probe detection.
Detection of TM in spiked samples by ultraviolet spectroscopy.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted carbon dots fluorescent sensing platform for visual detection of Thiophanate-methyl in fruits and vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, Gansu 730050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Sciences and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences and Engineering, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou, Gansu 730050, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Agriculture and Forestry, Longdong University, Qingyang, Gansu 745000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trimesic acid and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were employed as precursors to synthesize yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots (Y-G-CDs) by solvothermal synthesis for the sensitive detection of Thiophanate-methyl (TM) in real agricultural products. The Y-G-CDs probe could specifically recognize the TM primarily through π-π stacking. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching of the probe was ultimately dominated by the PET effect, based on the interaction between the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of the Y-G-CDs and the amino group of TM. A strong linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching of the probe and TM concentration in the range of 0–10 µmol/L was observed and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 50.7 nmol/L. Compared to the interference pesticides, the Y-G-CDs probe demonstrated exceptional selectivity toward TM, with satisfactory recoveries of 96.3 % − 104.2 % in spiked food samples. The Y-G-CDs probe enables simple pretreatment, cost-effective, and on-site detection of TM in fruits and vegetables with visual detection of the TM employing a smartphone-assisted sensing platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide residues resulting from overuse and abuse in foods and relevant matrices have posed a serious threat to public health in the world. Highly selective and sensitive detection of diverse pesticides is of great urgency and importance in food safety control (Fang et al., 2021). Pesticide contamination poses a potential hazard to the environment, organisms, and food safety due to the widespread and massive misuse of pesticides (Islam et al., 2020). Most of the applied pesticides ordinarily influx the terrestrial and aquatic food chains in the form of “residues” in the environment, where they become enriched and have potential, long-term adverse effects on human health (Regueiro et al., 2015). An effective monitoring of food contaminants is important to ensure food quality throughout the marketplace (M. Zhang et al., 2015). Thiophanate-methyl (TM), a broad-spectrum benzimidazole fungicide, is extensively applied in pre- and post-harvest for the control of a wide range of fruit and vegetable pathogens due to its relatively low cost and high antifungal efficiency(Malhat et al., 2021). The approved maximum residual limits (MRLs) of thiophanate-methyl in pear, orange, and tomato samples were less than 2 mg/kg (EU),2 mg/kg (EU), and 3 mg/kg (FAO/WHO), respectively. TM degrades slowly in the natural environment, and will not only remain on plants or fruits after its application but will also be flushed by rain into the aquatic environment spreading pollution quietly. TM can easily convert to carbendazim which has low acute toxicity but is carcinogenic to mammals and may affect reproduction. It is a suspected endocrine disruptor and can disrupt the endocrine system in mammals. In addition to the widely reported carcinogenic effects of TM, several studies have declared deleterious effects including disruption of mammalian genetic systems and metabolism (Feki et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2016). Currently, the residue of this pesticide in food has been strictly controlled at domestic and international. Therefore, it is necessary to explore a new method of detecting TM for the monitoring of food safety.

The contamination of TM has become a global problem, it is urgent to develop a highly sensitive assay for sensing pesticides in environmental samples to prevent the ecosystem from being destroyed (Weis et al., 2019). Various techniques have been applied to detect and identify pesticide residues, including liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (Liu et al., 2021), QUECHERS–HPLC–VWD (P. Li et al., 2020), fluorescence assay (FL) (Kim et al., 2021), high-performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) (Authority, 2016), Chromatography–Tandem Mass Spectrometry (H. Chen et al., 2014), ultraviolet (UV) (Jiao et al., 2014), high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry/mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS/MS) (Ahn et al., 2020), electrochemical assay (EC) (G.Y. Chen et al., 2021), Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) (Jiang et al., 2017), gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) (Chang et al., 2021), and thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Skowron et al., 2018). Although these assays have outstanding sensitivity and accuracy, some inevitable shortcomings confined their practical applications such as expensive equipment, complicated pre-treatment, and time-consuming operation, etc. Hence, there is an urgent demand to develop a low-cost, simple, time-consuming, efficient, and effective detection method of TM. Compared with the above-mentioned methods, Fluorescence (FL) analysis has attracted increasing attention because of their lower cost, simplicity of utility, quick response time, and real-time detection. Most importantly, it has great potential for on-site detection (Fu et al., 2022). Traditional laboratory-based sensing strategies for food contaminant detection are often limited because they are time-consuming and expensive and require trained personnel, which makes them unsuitable for routine sensing (Umapathi et al., 2022). Various portable detection technologies have been developed recently for on-site monitoring of pesticide residues in fruits and vegetables. These technologies include colorimetric, fluorescence, electrochemical, surface plasmon resonance, chemiluminescence, phosphorescence, microfluidic, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering techniques (Umapathi, et al.,2022). Alhammadi et al. developed a one-step lateral flow immunochromatographic assay (LFIA) for the simultaneous quantitative and qualitative detection of STR in pig serum and urine samples using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). To create LFIA test strips, a particular monoclonal antibody against streptomycin was coupled with AuNPs (Alhammadi, et al.,2023). Therefore, developing simple, low-cost, and rapid-sensing strategies and portable devices for the precise, efficient, rapid, and on-site detection of pesticide residues in agricultural fields is indeed necessary (Umapathi, et al., 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique luminescent properties, good biocompatibility, chemical stability, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis. In recent years, CDs based fluorescent sensing materials have gained more attention for the detection of pesticides (Rasheed, 2023; Xu et al., 2019). Excitingly, FL CDs as signal indicators can notably improve the sensing performance, rendering them highly compliable for pesticide detection, and the relevant high-quality reports have grown exponentially (X. Zhang et al., 2022). Benefiting from these merits, various CDs based sensors have been designed in the area of food safety for pesticide detection (Shi et al., 2019). Solvothermal carbonization provides a simple one-step experimental setup through high temperature and pressure (Tafreshi et al., 2020). The hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are popular because these methods are fast, easily scalable, cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and provide a high quantum yield of CDs with aqueous soluble properties (Tuerhong et al., 2017). Real-time analytical assays for CDs sensing using colorimetry and some efforts have been devoted to the development of cheaper and simpler colorimetric assays which are more suitable for point-of-care testing applications (Huang et al., 2021). Visualization refers to the technique of analyzing test results qualitatively or quantitatively directly with the naked eye, avoiding the need for expensive and complex instruments. Smartphone-assisted color analysis has appeared as advantageous advice considering measuring target analytes in resource-constrained circumstances in view of the fact that they are low-cost, rapid, conveyable, simple operation, and environmentally friendly (Guo et al., 2023). What’s more, CDs sensor combines with a smartphone which has high-quality cameras and good data analysis capability to capture and analyze fluorescent image color values for semi-quantitative visual detection of goal analytes.

In this work, we employed yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots, derived from Trimesic acid and o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), to construct a fluorescent probe to specifically identify TM. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the constructed fluorescent Y-G-CDs probe exhibited an emission peak at 530 nm when excited at 440 nm. Upon the addition of TM to the Y-G-CDs solution, a decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed. Furthermore, an increase in TM concentration resulted in a gradual change in the fluorescence color of the Y-G-CDs solution from yellow-green to green. By integrating the Y-G-CDs probe with a smartphone, we developed a smartphone-assisted sensing platform to detect TM by analyzing the RGB values of the Y-G-CDs' fluorescent colors (red, green, and blue channel values) using the built-in color analysis software. Consequently, the Y-G-CDs probe prepared in this study facilitates the visual detection of TM in food matrixes. Moreover, the carbon dots fluorescence quenching mechanism of the efficacy fluorescent response to TM in real agricultural products was investigated to facilitate the construction of visual fluorescence CDs probes in the measurement of containment in food.

Trimesic acid and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Ethanol was obtained from Tianli Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin). All experimental water was purified water. The pesticide standards (Carbendazim, Thiophanate-methyl, Procymidone, Dimethomorph, Chloramphenicol, Methamidophos, Thiabendazole, Dichlorvos, Clorpyrifos, Dimethoate) were acquired from the Aladdin Chemistry Co. ltd. (China). All the chemicals and reagents used were of analytical reagent grade without further purification. The solutions were stored in a refrigerator at 4℃.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) Talos F200S（FEI, USA) was used to acquire the TEM images. The absorption spectrum of MBC was measured by a double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Techcomp, Shanghai, China). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were characterized using a D8 (Bruker AXS, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of nanoparticles were determined by a Nicolet iS20 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy（XPS）was applied to analyze the qualitative, quantitative, and structural identification of the CDs (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescence measurements were carried out by F97pro (Lengguang, Shanghai, China) at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

Yellow-green-emissive carbon dots (Y-G-CDs) were prepared to refer to previous work with appropriate changes (He et al., 2021). Shortly, 1.000 g Trimesic acid and 0.500 g OPD were mixed and dissolved in 50.0 mL water and were sonicated for complete dissolution. The homogeneous solution was moved into a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 180 ℃ for 4 h. It was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min, and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, the obtained solution was stored at 4 ℃ for the following experiments.

The selectivity of the fluorescent nanoprobe to TM was evaluated by comparing the intensities of fluorescence response of the sensor to TM and other pesticides. In this scenario, various interfering pesticides, including Carbendazim, Thiophanate-methyl, Procymidone Dimethomorph, Chloramphenicol, Methamidophos, Thiabendazole, Dichlorvos, Chlorpyrifos, and Dimethoate, were examined through their influences on the fluorescence emissive of Y-G-CDs.

The feasibility and practicability of fluorescent sensors for the detection of TM in actual samples was evaluated by examining real samples including Pear, Orange, and Tomato. All real samples were purchased from a local market. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of TM (0, 4.0, 8.0 µmol/L) with a facile pre-treatment, then were chopped and homogenized separately. The spiked samples were mixed with 50.0 mL of acetonitrile and homogenized with a fierce shake for 5 min followed by resting for 30 min. Finally, the sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was treated in a water bath for 10 min, filtered through a 0.22 µm microfiltration membrane and then the resulting filtrate was dissolved in methanol. Eventually, the detection of TM used the fluorescent probe in real samples according to the above-mentioned procedure by using a fluorescence spectrometer.

The morphology of the synthesized Y-G-CDs was characterized by TEM to investigate the microstructure of d-CDs shown in Fig. 1 A and B, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 1A, the Y-G-CDs are spherical, and uniformly dispersed with the particle size ranging from 0.63 to 3.52 nm and an average particle size of 2.14 nm as shown in Fig. 1B. The FT-IR (Acquisition Parameters: Detector: DTGS KBr, Beam splitter: KBr, Light source: Infrared, Resolution: 4.000, Sampling gain: 8.0, Moving mirror speed: 0.4747 and Diaphragm: 80.00) spectra were shown in Fig. 1C. The characteristic bands of Y-G-CDs were observed at 3329 cm−1 (–OH), 2973 cm−1 ∼ 2886 cm−1 and 675 cm−1 (stretching of CH), 1706 cm−1 and 1379 cm−1 (asymmetric and symmetric telescopic vibrations CO). The peaks at 879 cm−1 and 1269 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching vibration of –NH2 and CO bonds. The band observed at 1502 cm−1 was due to CC backbone vibrations of the benzene ring and the CN stretching vibrations at 1087 cm−1 to 1045 cm−1 were also included. The above results indicate the presence of aromatic compound structures and hydrophilic groups in Y-G-CDs. Fig. 1D showed the XRD image of Y-G-CDs, from which it can be seen that a broad diffraction peak appears at 23.56°, indicating that Y-G-CDs had an amorphous structure. The presence of additional spurious peaks indicated that the synthesized carbon dots may be doped with other impurities.

Further elemental analysis of Y-G-CDs by XPS(Acquisition Parameters: Source Gun Type: Al K Alpha, Spot Size:500 um, Lens Mode: Standard, Analyser Mode: CAE: Pass Energy 30.0 eV and Energy Step Size: 0.100 eV). As illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The XPS full scan survey of Y-G-CDs and Y-G-CDs + TM exhibited some peaks of C1s (293.76 eV), O1s (538.23 eV) and N1s (403.49 eV) and C1s (293.76 eV), O1s (538.23 eV), N1s (403.49 eV) and S2p (171.37 eV), respectively. In Fig. 2B, the high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of Y-G-CDs presented their different peaks at 284.3, 284.8, 285.6, 289.0 and 291.3 eV which could be contributed to the CC, CC, CN/CO, CO and π-π* satellite in aromatic structures, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of N1s showed two different chemical states of nitrogen, pyridine N (398.9 eV) and pyrrole N (400.8 eV), (Fig. 2C). The high-resolution spectrum of O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) showed two bonds CO (531.9 eV) and CO (533.4 eV). In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of S2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2E) in Y-G-CDs + TM showed bond CS (167.9 eV), which confirmed the successful combination of Y-G-CDs to TM. These results indicated that Y-G-CDs have good water solubility, which is consistent with FTIR.

In order to analyze the optical properties of Y-G-CDs, they were first subjected to UV–vis absorption (Acquisition Parameters: Measurement method: ABS, Starting wavelength: 800 nm, End wavelength: 200 nm, Scanning speed: 400 nm/mi, Sampling interval: 1, Slit width: 1.5 and Fast optical range length: 10) and fluorescence spectroscopic exploration(Acquisition Parameters: Excitation wavelength: 440 nm, Emission onset wavelength: 300 nm, Emission termination wavelength: 800 nm, Scanning speed: 1000 nm/mi, Scanning interval: 1 nm and Gain: Medium). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Y-G-CDs solution in Fig. 2F showed two distinct exciton absorption bands, which are centered at 270 nm and 420 nm, attributed to the π-π* leaps of the C═C bond and the n-π* leaps of the C═O/CN bond, respectively. Among them, the exciton absorption band at 420 nm determines the source of green light emission from Y-G-CDs(Miao et al., 2018).The fluorescence spectrogram of Y-G-CDs indicated a fluorescence emission peak at 530 nm under the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, and the inset showed that the solution of Y-G-CDs was bright yellow-green under the 365 nm UV lamp.

Excitation emission, incubation time, and pH were optimized to achieve optimal detection performance of the Y-G-CDs probe toward TM. The fluorescence spectra of Y-G-CDs were examined at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3 A), revealing an increase in fluorescence emission intensity with an increased excitation wavelength range of 370–440 nm. However, a decrease was observed from 440 nm to 490 nm. In order to avoid mutual interference between excitation and emission intensity, 440 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for detection. The incubation time of the probe for TM detection was optimized using the fluorescence intensity value of the probe post-TM addition as an indicator. The impactof BR buffer solutions with varying pH (2–12) on the sensor response signals (Y-G-CDs) post-TM addition was investigated. In Fig. 3B, the Y-G-CDs exhibit stable fluorescence at pH 5–8. The fluorescence intensity of Y-G-CDs, which possessed multiple functional groups on their surface, can be significantly altered under strong acid or alkali conditions. Fig. 3C illustrated a significant decrease in the probe's fluorescence upon TM addition between 0 and 30 min, with a slight decrease beyond 30 min. Therefore, 30 min was chosen as the incubation time for subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence spectra of the sensor under optimal parameters were shown in Fig. 4 A, following the addition of varying TM concentrations (0–10 μmol/L). With the increase of TM concentration, the fluorescence of the sensor at 530 nm was obviously quenched. Furthermore, the fluorescence color of the sensor solution, when irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp, underwent a significant shift from yellow-green to green with increasing TM concentration, as depicted in Fig. 4A. Fig. 4B demonstrated a stronglinear correlation between F/F0 and the concentration of TM within the 0–10 μmol/L range, expressed by the linear equation F/F0 = -0.04135x + 0.98695 and the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9955. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 50.7 nmol/L using the formula LOD = 3σ/k (N = 6), which was lower than the maximum levels in pome fruit by EU (0.5 mg /kg, equivalent to 1.5 μmol/L). Table 1 compared the Y-G-CDs probe with previous TM detection methods using fluorescent sensors. The Y-G-CDs probe presented higher sensitivity and wider linear range than those probes reported in previous literature, suggesting its promising application potential for the detection of TM in agricultural products.

The Smartphone-assist platform reads color information from fluorescent images by capturing them and performing specific image processing and analysis were shown in Fig. 4C, which facilitates human vision in real-time and on-site measurement. As the increased concentration of TM in the sensor, an alteration in the fluorescence color of the sensor from yellow-green to green was observed. This can be applicable in visual detection using a smartphone-assisted sensing platform for the fluorescence colorimetric detection of TM. The constructed fluorescent sensor emitted fluorescence when excited by 365 nm UV lamps. The fluorescence data were corresponding to each concentration in Fig. 4A were imported into the CIE chromaticity diagram using software (CIE1931xy.V.1.6.0.2a) in Fig. 4D to obtain the corresponding chromaticity coordinates and the colors of the corresponding theories, which corresponded to the changes in the colors of the sensors post the addition of varying TM concentrations (Refer to the Fig. 4A). With the increase in TM concentration, its coordinate position changes did not pass through the red area, indicating that its red channel value (R) was basically unchanged and could be used as the reference channel value. The Y-G-CDs probe primarily exhibited changes in B and G (blue and green channel) values. As shown in Fig. 4E, there was a strong linear relationship between TM concentration in the range of 0–10 µmol/L and the value of B/G = 0.04591X + 0.2074. Where X is the concentration of TM in μmol/L; the coefficient of determination R2 of the standard curve is 0.9953 and the LOD was calculated to be 0.1306 μmol/L according to the 3σ rule. Consequently, the Y-G-CDs probe realized the requirement of visual detection of the TM indicating promising future development prospects.

To assess the specificity of the Y-G-CDs probe in recognizing TM, the impact of various interfering pesticides (Carbendazim, Thiophanate-methyl, Procymidone, Dimethomorph, Chloramphenicol, Methamidophos, Thiabendazole, Dichlorvos, Clorpyrifos and Dimethoate) on Y-G-CDs fluorescence under the same conditions were measured. The pesticides were used at a concentration of 10 μmol/L. Fig. 4F revealed that TM significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of Y-G-CDs, while the other nine interfering pesticides had minimal impact. These indicated that the synthesized Y-G-CDs could specifically recognize TM and trigger fluorescence quenching, potentially due to π-π stacking and the carboxyl groups. Furthermore, the abundant carboxyl groups through interacted with the amino group of TM, which ultimately dominated the fluorescence quenching through the PET effect. Consequently, the Y-G-CDs possessed excellent selectivity for the determination of TM over the other nine pesticides, implying great application potential in real samples.

To further assess the accuracy and reliability of the Y-G-CDs probe’s fluorescent properties for the detection of TM in real samples. Spiked recovery tests were carried out in actual agricultural samples (Pear, Orange, and Tomato) that are prone to TM residues. These tests were compared with the national standard ultraviolet spectrophotometry method to analyze the accuracy and feasibility of the constructed sensor for TM detection. As demonstrated in Table 2 , recovery of TM was in the range of 96.3–104.2 % with relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) lower than 6.7 %, confirming the successful detection of TM in complex food matrices by the Y-G-CDs probe. As indicated in Table 3 , the results of the Y-G-CDs probe detection method were not significantly different from those of the ultraviolet spectrophotometry method (p > 0.05), further suggesting the applicability of the Y-G-CDs probe for practical TM detection.

Every barber knows that the occurrence of FRET and IFE requires the overlap of the fluorescence emission or excitation spectrum of CDs with the absorption spectrum of the quencher. PET can be succinctly described as an electron transfer between CDs and the quencher, and the fluorescence lifetime of Y-G-CDs will be reduced by the quencher (Wei et al., 2021). Herein, aiming to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of TM on Y-G-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TM and fluorescence spectra of Y-G-CDs were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence spectrogram of Y-G-CDs indicated a fluorescence emission peak at 530 nm under the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, which did not intersect withthe UV–vis absorption peak of TM, primarily located at 200–300 nm. This observation led to the inference that FRET and IFE may not be the primary factors in TM’s recognition of Y-G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1, the FTIR spectrum of Y-G-CDs exhibited a cluster of peaks at 1502 cm−1 due to CC backbone vibrations of the benzene ring, which were attributed to the aromatic compounds (Han et al., 2021).

The characteristic bands of Y-G-CDs were observed at 3329 cm−1 –1269 cm−1, indicating that Y-G-CDs contain a large number of carboxyl and amino groups. The presence of these hydrophilic groups enhances the hydrophilicity of the Y-G-CDs. These results suggested that Y-G-CDs could recognize TM likely through π-π stacking. Subsequently,the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of Y-G-CDs can trigger a fluorescence quenching by reducing the surface state of Y-G-CDs and the PET effect through interaction with the amino group of TM. The high-resolution XPS is employed to further investigate the specific interaction. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in Y-G-CDs + TM were different from Y-G-CDs due to the emergence of a new peak at 291.3 eV, which accounted for the presence of bond π-π* satellite (referred to Fig. 2B). This π-π*satellite is due to the π-electron jump π → π* in the aromatic ring. The high-resolution spectra of N1s showed two different chemical states of nitrogen pyridine N and pyrrole N, (referred to Fig. 2C). It indicated that nitrogen is doped into Y-G-CDs through the amination reaction between amino and carboxyl groups on the benzene ring, and the doping of nitrogen effectively increases the electron cloud density on the surface of Y-G-CDs. Furthermore, the shift between the two chemical states of nitrogen after the addition of TM is attributed to the interaction between Y-G-CDs and TM. The high-resolution spectrum of the O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) of CO (531.9 eV) and CO (533.4 eV) demonstrated the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The above discussion further suggested that the Y-G-CDs specifically recognized the TM primarily through π-π stacking and the carboxyl groups. In addition, the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of Y-G-CDs through interacted with the amino group of TM, which ultimately dominated the fluorescence quenching through the PET effect.

This study presents a simple, sensitive, and selective fluorescence Y-G-CDs probe, designed for the dual-mode detection of TM in agricultural products via fluorescence and fluorescence colorimetric methods. The Y-G-CDs probe specifically recognized the TM primarily through π-π stacking. In addition, the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of Y-G-CDs interactwith the amino group of TM, which ultimately dominated the fluorescence quenching through the PET effect. The probe demonstrated excellentselectivity and a satisfactory recovery rate for TM detection in spiked food samples. Furthermore, the Y-G-CDs probe enables simple pretreatment, cost-effectiveness, and on-site visual detection of the TM employing a smartphone-assisted sensing platform, indicating significant potential forapplication prospects both in the field for rapid detection and in resource-poor areas. This study also provides a certain theoretical basis for future research on paper-based sensors. The Y-G-CDs probe has achieved visual detection of TM, However, testing methods that require instrumentation have been difficult to extend to the general public and resource-poor areas where expertise is lacking. Portable devices for on-site recognition of residual pesticides in vegetables and fruits face several challenges, especially in view of the economic benefits and ease of operation, the manufacture of paper-based sensors is necessary. Paper is an emerging analytical sensing platform due to its low cost, flexibility, disposability, and environmental friendliness. However, it is not enough to use the theoretical foundations of this study. We need to construct paper-based colorimetric sensors from the study of interfacial modulation of paper-based surfaces and high-energy trapping of target molecules. In addition, We need to continuously optimize and improve parameters and reaction schemes to improve paper-based sensor detection sensitivity, limit the diffusion of the target, and enhance the binding efficiency. We believe that the challenges mentioned above will be efficiently resolved by continuous innovations in electrochemical science, nanoscience, nanotechnology, and material science. We always firmly believe that with the support of more research in the future, mature integrated paper-based sensors will greatly facilitate people's productive lives.

A nanoprobe enable simple pretreatment, cost-effective and on-site detection of TM in fruits and vegetables with visual detection of the TM employing a simple device combined with a a smartphone-assisted sensing platform.

Yanxin Ma: methodology, data curation, writing-original draft. Ya Wang: methodology, review and editing. Hui Wang: methodology and editing. Fei Shang, Bo Yang: software. Yong Han: methodology, writing-review and editing.

Ya Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yanxin Ma: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Hui Wang: Project administration, Methodology. Fei Shang: Software. Bo Yang: Software. Yong Han: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32060571), West Light Foundation, Chinese Academy of Science (21JR7RM186), Science and Technology Department of Gansu Province (23CXGM0001, 22JR5RM204, 23CXGM0001).
